title: Adventures of Huckleberry Finn
author: Mark Twain

timeline.span: one antebellum summer (the year is approximate; Twain sets the story 'forty to fifty years ago', when slavery was still law along the river)

overture: One summer down the Mississippi on a raft - a runaway boy and Jim, an enslaved man running for his freedom - drawn on the real river, from a Missouri town to the deep South and the choice that damns Huck to save his friend.
sources: Mark Twain, Adventures of Huckleberry Finn (1884), Project Gutenberg #76; the Mississippi centreline from Natural Earth; St Petersburg = Hannibal, Missouri (standard Twain scholarship).

characters.name: Huck Finn
characters.role: A poor, unschooled, good-hearted boy who fakes his death and rafts down the Mississippi.

characters.name: Jim
characters.role: An enslaved man fleeing sale away from his wife and children - and the truest heart on the raft.

locations.name: St Petersburg, Missouri
locations.note: Huck's fictional home town, universally identified with Twain's own boyhood Hannibal, Missouri, on the Mississippi; coordinates are Hannibal.
locations.story: Huck's home, and the trap he flees. Newly rich from treasure and half-heartedly 'sivilized' by the Widow Douglas, he is stalked by his drunken Pap - who wants the money, and locks the boy up. Huck fakes his own murder to get free.
locations.quote: You don't know about me without you have read a book by the name of The Adventures of Tom Sawyer; but that ain't no matter.

image.caption: Hannibal, Missouri, from across the river, c.1904 - the real town behind Twain's St Petersburg.
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co., 1904 · Library of Congress · public domain

locations.name: Jackson's Island
locations.note: The wooded island in the Mississippi a couple of miles below the town where Huck hides; modelled on the real Glasscock's Island. Placed out on a real island in the channel, not the bank.
locations.story: The hiding place, and the meeting. Here Huck lies low - and finds Jim, who has run from Miss Watson rather than be sold away from his family. The two make the island home until the hunt closes in, then take to a raft.

image.caption: A Mississippi lumber raft riding the current between wooded shores - the kind of raft Huck and Jim build and slip away on. From Twain's own 'Life on the Mississippi', 1883.
image.credit: E. H. Garrett, 1883 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: St Louis, Missouri
locations.note: A real, named city the raft drifts past in the dark on the fifth night down; coordinates are the St Louis riverfront.
locations.story: The big city, passed by night - a blaze of lights on the black water, and gone. The raft keeps to the current, hiding by day and floating by night, bound for Cairo.
locations.quote: The fifth night we passed St. Louis, and it was like the whole world lit up.

image.caption: The levee at St Louis, 1890 - wagons, cordwood and moored sternwheelers, the Eads Bridge beyond. The city the raft drifts past in the dark.
image.credit: Robert Benecke, 1890 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Cairo, Illinois
locations.note: The real town at the confluence where the Ohio meets the Mississippi - 'the bottom of Illinois, where the Ohio River comes in'. Their planned turn-off, missed in the fog.
locations.story: The turning point they never reach. Here the Ohio joins the Mississippi, and here Huck and Jim mean to sell the raft and steam up the Ohio to the free States. In a thick fog they slip past Cairo unseeing - and Jim's road to freedom goes with it.
locations.quote: We would sell the raft and get on a steamboat and go way up the Ohio amongst the free States, and then be out of trouble.
locations.imageBlank: No dignified period view of Cairo, Illinois surfaced through the search; the missed confluence - the turning-point they never reach - keeps a logged blank rather than a mislabel.

locations.name: where the steamboat ran them down
locations.note: Unlocated in the text: Twain has them shove out after dark below Cairo and drift 'three hours and more' before an up-bound steamboat runs the raft down. Placed on the real channel a few miles above the Grangerford landing - close enough for Huck's 'long, slanting, two-mile crossing' to shore, far enough that the parting reads on the map.
locations.story: The hinge of the book. An up-bound steamboat bulls straight up the channel in thick weather and smashes through the raft; Huck and Jim go overboard on opposite sides. Huck swims for the shore and walks into the Grangerfords' feud; Jim saves the raft and hides it in a creek close by. They are apart until the feud is over.
locations.quote: as Jim went overboard on one side and I on the other, she come smashing straight through the raft

image.caption: A lower-Mississippi sternwheeler under way, c.1900 - a boat like the one that comes up the channel in the dark and smashes through the raft, parting Huck from Jim.
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co., c.1900 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: the Grangerford house
locations.note: The genteel feuding family's house is unlocated in the text; because the raft has overshot Cairo, scholars place it just below the Ohio's mouth. Point set on the real Mississippi centreline in that stretch.
locations.story: The aristocratic house where Huck is taken in after a steamboat runs down the raft. Kind, cultured people locked in a murderous feud with the Shepherdsons - which ends before Huck's eyes in a riverbank killing. He flees back to the mended raft and to Jim.
locations.imageBlank: The Grangerfords' house is Twain's invention and unlocated; the honest period images of such a place are the plantation-house genre scenes this project deliberately keeps out, so the house is a logged blank and the narration carries it.

locations.name: Bricksville, Arkansas
locations.note: A fictional 'one-horse' Arkansas river town; Twain sets it plainly on the Arkansas bank. Point placed on the river at a plausible Arkansas reach (~34.5N).
locations.story: A shabby Arkansas town where the two con men who have seized the raft - a self-styled duke and king - fleece the crowd with a crooked show. In the same dusty street Huck watches a harmless drunk shot dead for his taunts.

image.caption: The working riverfront of a lower-Mississippi levee town, c.1900 (Vicksburg) - the kind of shabby river town where the frauds put on their show.
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co., c.1900 · Library of Congress · public domain

locations.name: the Wilks town
locations.novelName: the town of the Wilks brothers
locations.note: The town of the Peter Wilks inheritance swindle is unnamed in the text; placed on the river below Bricksville (~34N) to keep the narrative order.
locations.story: Here the frauds try their cruellest swindle: posing as the English brothers of a dead man to rob his orphaned nieces of their inheritance. Sickened, Huck turns against the pair, helps expose them, and barely escapes back to the raft.

image.caption: Wooded bluffs above the lower Mississippi - the wild river country the raft slips through between the towns. Joseph Rusling Meeker, 1881.
image.credit: Joseph Rusling Meeker, 1881 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: the Phelps farm
locations.note: Silas and Sally Phelps's small cotton farm, 'two mile below' the fictional village of Pikesville - the novel's deepest-south point. Placed on the river near the Arkansas–Louisiana line (~33.5N).
locations.story: The end of the road. The king sells Jim here for forty dollars; Huck, alone, decides to damn himself rather than betray his friend - and, by chance, walks into the farm of Tom Sawyer's own relatives, who take him for Tom.
locations.quote: All right, then, I'll go to hell
locations.imageBlank: A small cotton farm deep in slave country; the honest period images of such places are the plantation-labour genre scenes this project deliberately keeps out, so the farm is a logged blank and the narration carries it.

movements.note: Huck fakes his own murder to escape his father and slips down to the island by canoe.
Jim has run too - from Miss Watson, who means to sell him down the river - and hides on the same island, where Huck finds him.
The two set off downriver on a raft, hiding by day and floating by night - free and easy on the water - bound for Cairo and the Ohio.
movements.routeNote: Down the Mississippi past St Louis toward Cairo, exactly as the raft goes in the text.
movements.routeSource: Down the real Mississippi, traced from Natural Earth's river centreline; Twain narrates the drift south, naming St Louis, the Ohio's mouth at Cairo, and the Arkansas bank.

movements.note: Past Cairo the current carries them on below the Ohio's mouth; they shove out after dark and drift on until a steamboat bulls up the channel and runs the raft down.
movements.routeNote: Below Cairo, deeper into slave country - the wrong way for Jim.
movements.routeSource: Down the real Mississippi, traced from Natural Earth's river centreline; Twain narrates the drift south, naming St Louis, the Ohio's mouth at Cairo, and the Arkansas bank.

movements.note: Huck dives clear, comes up calling for Jim, gets no answer, and strikes out with a plank on a long slanting crossing to the shore.
movements.routeSource: Down the real Mississippi, traced from Natural Earth's river centreline; Twain narrates the drift south, naming St Louis, the Ohio's mouth at Cairo, and the Arkansas bank.

movements.note: Jim comes up on the other side, saves the battered raft, and floats it down the channel to a creek near the Grangerford place to hide and wait.
movements.routeSource: Down the real Mississippi, traced from Natural Earth's river centreline; Twain narrates the drift south, naming St Louis, the Ohio's mouth at Cairo, and the Arkansas bank.

movements.note: Reunited, they push off again - until a duke and a king paddle out, seize the raft, and turn it to their swindling.
movements.routeNote: On down the Mississippi into Arkansas; the towns below Cairo are Twain's inventions, placed on the real river.
movements.routeSource: Down the real Mississippi, traced from Natural Earth's river centreline; Twain narrates the drift south, naming St Louis, the Ohio's mouth at Cairo, and the Arkansas bank.

movements.note: Downriver to a bigger fraud: the pair pass themselves off as a dead man's English brothers to rob his orphaned nieces.
movements.routeSource: Down the real Mississippi, traced from Natural Earth's river centreline; Twain narrates the drift south, naming St Louis, the Ohio's mouth at Cairo, and the Arkansas bank.

movements.note: Then the king sells Jim for forty dollars, and Huck follows the trail to the Phelps farm to get him back.
movements.routeSource: Down the real Mississippi, traced from Natural Earth's river centreline; Twain narrates the drift south, naming St Louis, the Ohio's mouth at Cairo, and the Arkansas bank.

story.title: A boy in a river town
story.narration: Meet Huck Finn - poor, unschooled, and good-hearted - in the little Mississippi town of St Petersburg. Newly rich from a treasure and half-heartedly being 'sivilized' by the Widow Douglas, he is hunted by his drunken father, who wants the money and locks him up.
To get free of his violent Pap, Huck stages his own murder and slips off by canoe to a wooded island just down the river.
On the island he finds Jim, who has run from Miss Watson rather than be sold south, away from his wife and children. Two runaways, they throw in together - and it is a hanging matter to help him.

story.title: The hunt closes in
story.narration: They make the island home - until Huck, slipping into town in disguise, hears that men mean to hunt the island for Jim that very night. They load a raft and shove off into the dark current.

Downriver they go, hiding by day, floating by night - no home like a raft, and the two of them free and easy on the water. The plan: reach Cairo, sell the raft, and take a steamboat up the Ohio to the free States.

story.title: Cairo, missed in the fog
story.narration: At Cairo the Ohio opens north to the free States - Jim's whole road to freedom. But a thick fog swallows the river; they drift past the mouth unseeing, the turn dissolves in the mist, and the current carries them on, deeper into slave country.

Below Cairo the current carries them on, deeper into slave country. Then, in the dark, a steamboat bulls straight up the channel and smashes through the raft - and the two go overboard on opposite sides.
Huck dives, comes up calling for Jim, and gets no answer. He strikes out with a plank on a long slanting crossing, lands, and walks a quarter of a mile to the fine house of the Grangerfords.
Jim came up on the other side of the river.
He saves the battered raft and floats it down to a creek close by, lying low aboard, waiting for the boy he half fears is drowned.

story.title: The feud
story.narration: The Grangerfords take him in - courtly, cultured, and locked in a generations-old feud with the Shepherdsons that ends, before Huck's eyes, in a killing on the riverbank. Sick at heart, he finds Jim and the mended raft, and they slip away.

Back to the river's peace - until two con men climb aboard: a self-styled 'duke' and 'king' who seize the raft and take command. From here they run the show, and Huck and Jim are dragged along on their swindles.

story.title: Bricksville
story.narration: At a shabby Arkansas town the frauds stage a crooked show and fleece the crowd; in the same street Huck watches a harmless drunk shot dead by a gentleman he had taunted. The river world is turning cruel.

Downriver the duke and king try a crueller swindle yet: passing themselves off as the English brothers of a dead man to rob his orphaned nieces. Jim rides tied on the raft, painted up as a 'sick Arab' so he can be left aboard.

story.title: Huck turns
story.narration: Sickened by their cruelty to the kind Wilks girls, Huck turns against the frauds, hides the stolen gold, and helps expose them - then barely makes it back to the raft as the town boils over.
Then the king does the unforgivable and sells Jim for forty dollars. Huck traces him to the Phelps farm, deep in the cotton country - and there, alone, he must choose.

story.title: 'I'll go to hell'
story.narration: Huck writes the letter that would hand Jim back to slavery - then remembers Jim's watch-keeping and kindness on the river, tears it up, and chooses his friend over his own soul: 'All right, then, I'll go to hell.' It is the moral heart of the book.

story.title: Free, and lighting out
story.narration: By chance the farm belongs to Tom Sawyer's relatives, who take Huck for Tom. Tom turns Jim's rescue into a game - until word comes that Miss Watson had freed Jim in her will. Huck, dreading being 'sivilized' again, means to light out for the Territory.
